Long ago, before the young world knew the passage of time, the Moon, named Lunaris, was perfectly smooth. She was a sphere of flawless, radiant polished pearl. Lunaris was deeply vain. Instead of casting her glowing light upon the dark, shivering Earth below, she hoarded her brilliance, using it only to admire her own pristine reflection in the deep cosmic seas.

The Earth wept in darkness, and its cries reached Aether, the ancient and powerful spirit of the night sky. Aether pleaded with Lunaris to share her luminescence, but she simply turned her smooth face away, scoffing at the muddy world beneath her.

Angered by her profound selfishness, Aether reached into the very fabric of the Milky Way. He scooped up handfuls of heavy, jagged stardust and hurled the meteorites at the vain Moon. The burning star-stones struck her flawless surface, leaving deep, dark craters and jagged, permanent scars.

Shattered and humbled, Lunaris wept. Her perfect reflection was forever ruined. As a punishment, Aether bound her to orbit the Earth for eternity. Now, her scarred face serves as an eternal reminder of her vanity, and her fragmented, gentle light falls upon the world, guiding lost travelers through the endless dark.

Aethel commanded him to run across the highest, sharpest peaks of the mortal realm, brushing his tail against the lower edge of the heavens. He danced through the stratosphere, weaving glowing ribbons of light that chased away the oppressive shadows.

Today, when the winter sky shimmers with vibrant hues, it is simply Vesper, running his eternal race to keep the darkness at bay.

Seeing the mortals marvel at Vesper’s gift, his sister, a colossal white wolf named Luma, felt a profound calling to aid the heavens further.

Luma possessed a thick coat spun from pure, concentrated starlight and the glowing ashes of ancient suns. She approached the Moon Goddess, Aethel, and asked to build a bridge for the lost spirits of the mortal world. With Aethel’s blessing, Luma bounded into the darkest, emptiest stretches of the celestial sea. 

As she sprinted, the great wolf shook her magnificent coat violently. Billions of glowing silver hairs and specks of radiant dust cascaded from her fur, clinging to the heavy obsidian canvas of space. She ran in a massive, sweeping spiral until her brilliant coat was entirely gone, having given away all her warmth and light.

Aethel, moved by Luma’s sacrifice, transformed the shivering wolf into the North Star. Today, the glowing path of stardust she left behind is known as the Milky Way, guiding travelers home.

Myth: In the dawn of creation, the night sky possessed no stars. There was only Solis, the golden Sun, and his most prized possession: a colossal crystal mirror suspended high in the heavens. Solis was profoundly vain. While he spent his daylight hours bathing the world in heat, at night he would turn his radiant rays backward, illuminating only his grand mirror to admire his own blazing reflection in the dark abyss.

Below, the world plunged into absolute, terrifying darkness each evening. Mortals stumbled and fell, crying out for a sliver of guidance. Hearkening to their desperate pleas, the Great Wind, Zephyrus, approached Solis, begging him to angle the mirror to share a fraction of his nightly radiance. Solis merely laughed, refusing to obscure his own reflection for the mud-bound creatures.

Enraged by such arrogance, Zephyrus drew in a breath that emptied the oceans' gales and blew with cataclysmic force. The mighty wind struck the crystal mirror, shattering it into a billion radiant fragments. 

The shards scattered across the obsidian canvas of the sky, catching the residual light of the sun. The shattered fragments became the stars, forming gentle constellations that forever guide wanderers through the darkest nights.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: In the youth of the cosmos, the heavens were vast and entirely devoid of water. All the moisture of the universe was hoarded by an ancient Ice Titan named Caelum, who kept it frozen within a colossal, crystalline goblet. Caelum was deeply vain and solitary. He spent the eons gazing into the perfectly smooth, polished ice, admiring his own glittering reflection while the worlds below withered into dry, cracked husks. 

The deeply parched Earth wept dry dust, its cries reaching Ignis, the great cosmic Firebird. Pitying the dying realms, Ignis flew to Caelum’s fortress and begged for a single drop of frost to soothe the blistering worlds. The Titan merely scoffed, refusing to mar his perfect frozen mirror. 

Enraged by this cruelty, Ignis ascended to the highest peak of the heavens, folded her wings, and plummeted like a burning meteor. She crashed directly into the giant’s frozen goblet. The impact shattered the enchanted ice into millions of jagged, glowing fragments. 

These frozen shards were cast across the dark void, becoming the first comets. Now, they streak eternally through the abyss, their tails of melting ice bringing the promise of water and life to the farthest, thirsting corners of the universe.
